---
title: "About"
---

I'm a GIS developer and public-health data analyst, and lately more of an
epidemiologist. My degree is in geography, and I've spent a bit over a decade
building and analyzing large health and geospatial datasets across public-health
surveillance, healthcare, and federal hazard modeling. Now I'm putting that
toward epidemiology, mostly surveillance and environmental data and the spatial
and temporal structure that ordinary regression tends to miss. I'm based in
Centennial, Colorado.

## Now

I'm finishing an MPH in Epidemiology at the University of Memphis. My thesis is a
Bayesian spatiotemporal study of West Nile virus mosquito surveillance across
California, looking at how environmental covariates, surveillance effort, and
space-time patterns relate to infection rates in mosquito pools. Because the
surveillance data is access-restricted, I started with the validation rather
than the analysis: a simulation framework that checks whether each candidate
model actually recovers a known effect at its stated uncertainty, before any of
them see the real data.

I also work as a contractor developing GIS and risk-modeling software for FEMA's
Hazus natural-hazard loss-estimation program. The day-to-day is Python, C#/C++,
and SQL Server against ArcGIS-based data and modeling workflows: data
engineering, hazard and loss logic, and the testing that has to happen when you
change a model people depend on.

Before this I was a GIS analyst in healthcare at DaVita Kidney Care, and during
the pandemic I built COVID-19 surveillance databases, ETL pipelines, and public
dashboards for a Colorado health department.

## How I work

A few things I keep coming back to:

- **Surveillance data has a process behind it.** Where and how hard people looked
  shapes what shows up, so detection and effort belong in the model, not in a
  footnote.
- **Check new code against something that already works.** New spatial routines
  get compared to PySAL or R packages; new models get run on simulated data with
  a known answer before they touch anything real.
- **Keep it reproducible.** I'd rather write a pipeline than a one-off script,
  and I keep environments someone else can actually rebuild.
- **Be honest about what the analysis supports.** I'd sooner write down a
  limitation than round a result up.

## Interests

My interests run across spatial and computational epidemiology, environmental
health, public-health GIS, and applied statistics. I'm especially drawn to
vector-borne disease surveillance, spatial cluster detection, and the
methodological headaches that come up when surveillance data is sparse or uneven.

## Contact

- Email: [ross@rwardrup.com](mailto:ross@rwardrup.com)
- GitHub: [github.com/minorsecond](https://github.com/minorsecond)
- LinkedIn: [linkedin.com/in/robert-wardrup](https://www.linkedin.com/in/robert-wardrup/)
- [Curriculum vitae](cv.qmd)
